name: Angrboða
shortDescription: Angrboða is a jötunn (giantess) in Norse mythology, best known as the consort of Loki and the mother of monstrous offspring.
description: Angrboða's name translates to 'she who announces sorrow' or 'sorrow-bringer,' foreshadowing the calamitous role her children play in Norse cosmology, particularly during Ragnarök. She resides in Jötunheimr, the realm of the giants, and her association with Loki is a significant point in Norse myth, representing a union that produces beings disruptive to the order of the gods. Her children include the wolf Fenrir, the serpent Jörmungandr, and Hel, the ruler of the underworld. These three figures are pivotal in the events leading up to Ragnarök, the twilight of the gods. Fenrir is destined to devour Odin, Jörmungandr encircles Midgard (the world of humans) and poisons the land and sea during Ragnarök, and Hel presides over the realm of the dead, a place of considerable importance in Norse belief regarding the afterlife. Angrboða's role highlights the Norse preoccupation with fate and the inevitability of destruction. Her connection to Loki also underscores the ambiguous nature of Loki, a figure who exists both within and outside the Aesir, capable of both aiding and undermining the gods. She represents chaos and the untamed forces of nature, contributing to the overall sense of impending doom that permeates Norse mythology. Her existence challenges the established order of Asgard and symbolizes the ever-present threat from the untamed wilderness beyond its borders. The lack of extensive narratives directly involving Angrboða suggests her importance lies primarily in her role as the mother of these monstrous figures.

sources.title: Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
sources.author: John Lindow
